prior.title: Singapore Maritime Decarbonisation Blueprint Implementation Regulations
prior.summary: The Maritime and Port Authority of Singapore (MPA) released detailed implementation regulations in November 2023 for its maritime decarbonization blueprint. The regulations establish mandatory green shipping corridors, require ships calling at Singapore ports to use cleaner fuels on designated routes by 2025, and introduce a Maritime Singapore Green Finance Incentive Scheme. Port fees will be adjusted based on vessel emission performance starting 2024.
prior.why_matters: Freight forwarders routing cargo through Singapore face new compliance requirements for vessel emissions and potential cost changes through emission-based port fees. Green corridor requirements may limit vessel choices on key Asia-Europe and Asia-Americas routes, affecting service options and pricing. Forwarders may need to work with clients to adjust supply chain routing to meet sustainability requirements while maintaining cost competitiveness.
prior.full_brief:
# Singapore Maritime Decarbonisation Blueprint: Implementation Regulations and Programmes

## Section 1 — Purpose and Scope of This Document

This document synthesises available sourced intelligence on the Maritime Singapore Decarbonisation Blueprint ("the Blueprint"), published by the Maritime and Port Authority of Singapore (MPA) in 2022 and progressively implemented through regulatory mandates, incentive programmes, and industry engagement initiatives through to 2025–2026.

**Convention notes:**
- The workspace is referenced throughout as "the workspace" or by operational role. No workspace entity is named.
- All binding-obligation language is either span-grounded as a FACT with inline citation, recast as descriptive prose tracking source text, or prefixed with an analysis label.
- Items requiring legal interpretation are labeled *Legal Confirmation Required:*
- The primary source (item source_id: c49414da-7c9e-45cc-a629-f138166ecda5) is the MPA Blueprint page at https://www.mpa.gov.sg/maritime-singapore/sustainability/maritime-singapore-decarbonisation-blueprint. Corroborating sources are cited inline throughout.
- **Source gap noted:** The primary source URL supplied (https://mpa.gov.sg/media-centre/details/mpa-releases-detailed-implementation-plan-for-maritime-decarbonisation) returned a 404 error. No detailed implementation plan content was retrievable from that URL. The MOT source (https://www.mot.gov.sg/what-we-do/green-transport/maritime-environment-responsibility/) returned a 403 error. All claims are grounded in the five remaining accessible source blocks.
- **Scope limitation:** This brief covers the Blueprint's structure, seven focus areas, incentive programmes under the Maritime Singapore Green Initiative (MSGI), harbour craft regulatory mandates, and Singapore's international positioning. It does not cover enacted domestic legislation with penalty provisions, as no such sourced text was retrievable from the provided source blocks. Required regulatory slots are addressed below, with explicit gaps where facts are not available.

**Jurisdictional scope:** Singapore — Port of Singapore, Singapore Registry of Ships (SRS), MPA-licensed harbour craft, and ocean-going vessels calling at the Port of Singapore.

**Format:** Regulatory Fact Document (item_type: framework/initiative).

---

## Section 2 — What This Regulation Is and Why It Applies to the Workspace

**What it is:**

The Maritime Singapore Decarbonisation Blueprint: Working Towards 2050 is a strategic policy framework developed by the MPA in consultation with industry partners. It was published in 2022 and charts long-term decarbonisation strategies for Maritime Singapore. The Blueprint "will contribute to Singapore's commitments under the UN's 2030 Sustainable Development Agenda, Paris Agreement and the Initial IMO Strategy on reduction of GHG emissions from ships." *Source: COS 2022 Media Factsheet — Maritime Singapore Decarbonisation Blueprint: Working Towards 2050, MPA, 09 March 2022. https://www.mpa.gov.sg/media-centre/details/cos-2022---media-factsheet---maritime-singapore-decarbonisation-blueprint-working-towards-2050

The Blueprint "takes on board the recommendations made by the International Advisory Panel on Maritime Decarbonisation, and inputs from a two-month long public consultation and sector-specific consultations." *Source: COS 2022 Media Factsheet, MPA, 09 March 2022. https://www.mpa.gov.sg/media-centre/details/cos-2022---media-factsheet---maritime-singapore-decarbonisation-blueprint-working-towards-2050

The Blueprint is not a single enacted statute with penalty provisions. It is a policy document that drives specific regulatory mandates (notably for harbour craft from 2030), incentive programmes (MSGI), and industry engagement. *Analytical inference:* Workspaces with ocean-going vessel operations calling at Singapore, with vessels on the SRS, or with harbour craft operations in Singapore waters are the primary addressable entities. Freight forwarders and cargo principals transiting through Singapore face indirect exposure through vessel operators' compliance costs.

**Why it applies to the workspace:**

*Operational implication:* For workspaces operating as freight forwarders routing ocean cargo through the Port of Singapore — one of the world's largest bunkering hubs — the Blueprint's fuel transition programme (methanol, ammonia, biofuels) will progressively alter bunker fuel pricing and availability on Singapore-calling trade lanes. The workspace's cost base on Asia-Europe, transpacific, and intra-Asia ocean corridors is exposed to the cost pass-through from vessel operators complying with Singapore's incentive and mandate structure.

*Operational implication:* For workspaces moving time-critical cargo (live events equipment, fine art, luxury goods, film and TV production freight) via air primary / ocean secondary through Singapore, the harbour craft decarbonisation mandate has potential port-side handling cost implications from 2030 onward as harbour craft operators face new energy requirements.

**Effective date (required slot):**

The Blueprint was published on 09 March 2022. *Source: COS 2022 Media Factsheet, MPA, 09 March 2022. https://www.mpa.gov.sg/media-centre/details/cos-2022---media-factsheet---maritime-singapore-decarbonisation-blueprint-working-towards-2050

The MSGI refresh, including the Green Ship Programme (GSP), Green Craft Programme (GCP), and Green Port Infrastructure Programme (GPIP), took effect from 1 January 2025. *Source: Maritime Singapore Green Initiative, MPA. https://www.mpa.gov.sg/maritime-singapore/sustainability/maritime-singapore-green-initiative

**Primary compliance deadline (required slot — harbour craft mandate):**

"From 2030 onwards, all new harbour crafts must meet one of three criteria: they must be fully electric, compatible with B100 biofuel, or net-zero fuels (NZFs) ready." *Source: MPA-SSA Briefing Unites Harbour Craft Stakeholders To Meet Decarbonisation Goals, Singapore Shipping Association, 11 September 2025. https://www.ssa.org.sg/mpa-ssa-briefing-unites-harbour-craft-stakeholders-to-meet-decarbonisation-goals/

The port terminal target of at least 60% reduction in absolute emissions from 2005 levels carries a 2030 deadline, with net zero by 2050. The harbour craft target is a 15% reduction in absolute emissions from 2021 levels by 2030, and 50% reduction from 2030 levels by 2050. *Source: COS 2022 Media Factsheet, MPA, 09 March 2022. https://www.mpa.gov.sg/media-centre/details/cos-2022---media-factsheet---maritime-singapore-decarbonisation-blueprint-working-towards-2050

**Penalty / enforcement provision (required slot):**

*Specific penalty figures and enforcement provisions for non-compliance with Blueprint targets not available from primary sources as of June 2026.* The Blueprint is a strategic framework; the source blocks do not contain enacted statutory penalty text. *Legal Confirmation Required:* whether specific harbour craft mandate non-compliance triggers fines, licence suspension, or other enforcement under the Prevention of Pollution of the Sea Act or Merchant Shipping Act requires counsel review of MPA circulars and the relevant Singapore statutes.

---

## Section 3 — Issues Requiring Immediate Action

**MONITORING | Harbour Craft Operator Mandate — 2030 Deadline Approaching**

The 2030 new-build mandate for harbour craft (fully electric, B100-compatible, or NZF-ready) was confirmed at the September 2025 Joint MPA-SSA Briefing. *Source: MPA-SSA Briefing, SSA, 11 September 2025. https://www.ssa.org.sg/mpa-ssa-briefing-unites-harbour-craft-stakeholders-to-meet-decarbonisation-goals/

*Operational implication:* Workspaces that commission port-side harbour craft services in Singapore (towage, bunkering craft, service launches) from 2030 onward will face a counterparty fleet that is being progressively required to transition to higher-cost propulsion systems. Port-side handling cost increases should be anticipated in commercial planning for Singapore-routing contracts extending beyond 2028.

Action: Review Singapore port-side service contracts for harbour craft services. Confirm whether existing contract structures allow for fuel-transition surcharge pass-through. Flag for legal and commercial review.

*Re-check window: Q4 2026 — MPA's decarbonisation taskforce (announced September 2025) is expected to produce co-developed policy outputs; monitor for regulatory instruments formalising the mandate.*

---

**MONITORING | MSGI Green Ship Programme — Incentive Window to 31 December 2027**

The Green Ship Programme provides "up to 100% concessions on initial registration fees and annual tonnage taxes" for SRS vessels meeting qualifying criteria, and "up to 100% port dues concession to ocean-going vessels calling at the Port of Singapore" meeting fuel/technology criteria. Both programmes run "from 1 January 2025 until 31 December 2027." *Source: Maritime Singapore Green Initiative, MPA. https://www.mpa.gov.sg/maritime-singapore/sustainability/maritime-singapore-green-initiative

*Operational implication:* Ocean carriers calling Singapore and seeking port dues concessions have a defined window through end-2027 to qualify vessels. Post-2027 incentive structure has not been sourced.

Action: If the workspace has commercial relationships with carriers operating Singapore-registered vessels or calling Singapore regularly, confirm whether those carriers are enrolled in the GSP/GCP. This affects the carrier's cost base on Singapore trade lanes and may affect freight rate negotiations post-2027.

---

**MONITORING | Methanol Bunkering Regulatory Framework — In Development**

"Insights from the EOI submissions will inform the development of the methanol bunkering regulatory framework to ensure the safe and efficient supply of methanol." *Source: Singapore gears up to meet net-zero needs of shipping, MPA, 17 April 2024. https://www.mpa.gov.sg/media-centre/details/singapore-gears-up-to-meet-net-zero-needs-of-shipping/

*Analytical inference:* The methanol bunkering regulatory framework was in development as of April 2024. No sourced publication date for the finalised framework has been identified from the provided source blocks. This is a FORWARD-INTELLIGENCE item: the framework's publication will trigger operational requirements for methanol bunker operators and, downstream, affect fuel cost and availability signals for carriers calling Singapore.

Action: Monitor MPA Port Marine Circulars for methanol bunkering regulatory framework publication. Expected to precede meaningful commercial-scale methanol demand, which MPA estimates "can potentially exceed one million tons per annum (MTPA) before 2030." *Source: MPA, 17 April 2024. https://www.mpa.gov.sg/media-centre/details/singapore-gears-up-to-meet-net-zero-needs-of-shipping/

*Re-check window: Monitor quarterly. Analytical inference: given the 2030 commercial demand estimate and EOI completion by early 2024, framework publication is likely in the 2025–2027 window.*

---

**WINDOW CLOSING | Green Craft Programme and Green Energy and Technology Programme — Application Window**

The Green Craft Programme port dues concession for newbuild harbour craft applies to "newbuild harbour craft registered from 1 January 2025 until 31 December 2027." The Energy Efficiency Grant (base tier) application window runs "until 31 March 2027." *Source: Maritime Singapore Green Initiative, MPA. https://www.mpa.gov.sg/maritime-singapore/sustainability/maritime-singapore-green-initiative

Action: If the workspace owns or operates MPA-licensed harbour craft in Singapore, confirm eligibility for port dues concession and EEG co-funding before 31 March 2027 (EEG) and 31 December 2027 (GCP port dues). Current rates of available co-funding under the EEG advanced tier are publicly stated at up to 70% for electric forklifts; broader vessel-level co-funding figures are "not publicly available" from the source blocks for all equipment categories.

---

## Section 4 — How the Workspace Sits in the Compliance Chain

The Blueprint and its implementing programmes address multiple supply-chain roles simultaneously. The workspace's position depends on its specific role in each transaction touching Singapore.

**Ocean freight forwarder (cargo principal's agent):**
*Analytical inference:* As a freight forwarder, the workspace is not a direct obligated party under the vessel-level requirements of the Blueprint (vessel operators, SRS-registered shipowners, and harbour craft operators bear direct obligations). However, the workspace sits in the commercial chain between cargo principals and ocean carriers; fuel transition costs incurred by carriers under Singapore's incentive and mandate structure will flow through as freight rate adjustments. The workspace's ability to pass these through to clients depends on contract terms.

**Importer / Exporter (for specific transaction types):**
*Analytical inference:* Where the workspace acts as importer of record for cargo transiting Singapore, no direct compliance obligation under the Blueprint's current framework has been identified. The Blueprint's carbon accounting focus area (Focus Area 7) may over time produce reporting requirements that extend to cargo principals — but no such enacted requirement is sourced from the available source blocks.

**Supply chain position — harbour craft services:**
*Operational implication:* If the workspace commissions harbour craft services directly (e.g., lightering, vessel-to-vessel transfer, port service launches) in Singapore, the harbour craft operator's decarbonisation compliance costs from 2030 may be passed through to service pricing. Contract structures for port-side services extending beyond 2028 should reflect this transition risk.

*Legal Confirmation Required:* Whether the workspace, in any of its roles (forwarder, importer, exporter), faces direct reporting or registration obligations under the Blueprint's carbon accounting and green financing focus area (Focus Area 7) requires review of any enacted MPA circulars or statutory instruments implementing that focus area.

---

## Section 6 — Anticipated Authoritative Guidance and Pending Regulatory Events

**MONITORING | MPA Decarbonisation Taskforce — Policy Co-Development**

At the September 2025 Joint MPA-SSA Briefing, "SSA's Decarbonisation Committee Chair, Mr. Lee Keng Lin announced that MPA will form a decarbonisation taskforce to shape and co-develop policies and practical solutions with industry partners." *Source: MPA-SSA Briefing, SSA, 11 September 2025. https://www.ssa.org.sg/mpa-ssa-briefing-unites-harbour-craft-stakeholders-to-meet-decarbonisation-goals/

- **Phase/status:** Announced September 2025. Formation confirmed; outputs and timeline not yet published in the source blocks.
- **Design/intent:** Co-develop policies for harbour craft decarbonisation, addressing concerns raised at the briefing: technology readiness, financial support, crew training, and policy inclusivity for businesses of all sizes.
- **Participants:** MPA, SSA, and industry partners (harbour craft operators, tug service providers confirmed as attendees).
- **Expected timing:** *Analytical inference:* Taskforce outputs are likely to precede or inform the 2030 new-build mandate implementation. Re-check window: Q2 2026 and Q4 2026 for policy instrument releases.
- **What it investigates:** Whether and how to extend financial support mechanisms for low/zero-emission harbour craft transition beyond current MSGI incentives; workforce conversion programmes; charging infrastructure deployment.

---

**MONITORING | IMO Mid-Term Measures — Goal-Based Marine Fuel Standard and GHG Pricing Mechanism**

At the April 2024 MPA conference, IMO Secretary-General Arsenio Dominguez stated: "We are on track to adopt mid-term measures by late 2025 to cut GHG emissions, to reach net zero targets. These mid-term measures, which are currently being discussed by Member States, include a goal-based marine fuel standard, and a pricing mechanism for maritime GHG emissions." *Source: Singapore gears up to meet net-zero needs of shipping, MPA, 17 April 2024. https://www.mpa.gov.sg/media-centre/details/singapore-gears-up-to-meet-net-zero-needs-of-shipping/

- **Phase/status:** As stated in April 2024, IMO Member State discussions were ongoing with a stated adoption target of "late 2025."
- **Design/intent:** A goal-based marine fuel standard and a pricing mechanism for maritime GHG emissions, targeting net-zero GHG by or around 2050, with 2030 checkpoint (at least 20% GHG reduction, striving for 30%) and 2040 checkpoint (at least 70%, striving for 80%).
- **Participants:** IMO Member States; Singapore confirmed as active participant at MEPC 80 (July 2023).
- **What it triggers for the workspace:** If adopted, a GHG pricing mechanism will add a cost layer to ocean freight on all trade lanes, not only Singapore-calling routes. *Operational implication:* Workspaces routing ocean freight through Singapore — and globally — should anticipate a fuel-cost and carbon-levy pass-through from vessel operators once IMO mid-term measures are adopted. The timing of this pass-through depends on the finalised instrument.

*Re-check window: Monitor MEPC sessions in 2026 for adoption status. Note: the stated "late 2025" adoption target in the April 2024 source is a stated schedule and constitutes a FACT; whether adoption occurred by that date is not confirmed in the source blocks and requires external verification.*

---

**MONITORING | Methanol Bunkering Regulatory Framework — Publication Pending**

As noted in Section 3. No publication date is available from the source blocks. *Analytical inference:* The framework's publication will affect the safety and operational requirements for methanol bunkering operators in Singapore and, downstream, the cost and availability of methanol as a bunker fuel for methanol-capable vessel operators calling Singapore.

---

## Section 7 — Threshold Questions

**Question 1: Does the Blueprint's harbour craft mandate apply to the workspace's Singapore port operations?**

The mandate applies to "all new harbour crafts" registered from 2030 onwards. The question of whether the workspace directly operates MPA-licensed harbour craft, or only commissions third-party harbour craft services, determines whether the workspace bears a direct compliance obligation or only an indirect cost-pass-through exposure.

*Legal Confirmation Required:* Whether the workspace, in any of its operational configurations in Singapore, holds an MPA harbour craft licence or operates as a principal for licence purposes, or is solely a commercial counterparty to licensed operators, requires review of the workspace's Singapore operating agreements and MPA licence status.

**Question 2: Does the MSGI Green Ship Programme apply to vessels the workspace charters or nominates?**

The GSP applies to SRS-registered vessels and ocean-going vessels calling Singapore. The workspace, as freight forwarder, does not typically own vessels. *Analytical inference:* The commercial relevance is whether the carriers the workspace nominates are enrolled in the GSP and whether that affects their port dues (and thus their freight rates on Singapore-calling services).

**Question 3: Does Focus Area 7 (Carbon Accounting and Green Financing) create reporting obligations for freight forwarders?**

The Blueprint's Focus Area 7 covers "carbon awareness, carbon accounting and green financing." The source blocks describe this as a "green maritime finance hub" objective. *Specific enacted reporting obligations for freight forwarders under Focus Area 7 not available from primary sources as of June 2026.* The GAP programme under MSGI focuses on "raising awareness on and developing industry capabilities in carbon accounting and management." *Source: Maritime Singapore Green Initiative, MPA. https://www.mpa.gov.sg/maritime-singapore/sustainability/maritime-singapore-green-initiative

*Legal Confirmation Required:* Whether any enacted MPA circular or Singapore statute implementing Focus Area 7 creates direct carbon reporting obligations for freight forwarders or cargo principals operating in Singapore requires counsel review.

---

## Section 8 — Substantive Requirements

### 8.1 Seven Focus Areas of the Blueprint

The Blueprint outlines the following seven focus areas and targets, cited verbatim from the source:

1. **Port terminals:** "Transit towards a low-carbon future through the adoption of cleaner energy, automation, and digitalisation, to achieve: At least 60% reduction in absolute emissions from 2005 levels by 2030 [and] Net zero emissions by 2050."
2. **Domestic harbour craft:** "All harbour craft will operate on low-carbon energy solutions by 2030 and full electric propulsion and net zero fuels by 2050, to achieve: 15% reduction in absolute emissions from 2021 levels by 2030 [and] 50% reduction in absolute emissions from 2030 levels by 2050."
3. **Future marine fuels, bunkering standards and infrastructure:** "Be ready for a multi-fuel bunkering transition to support the future of international shipping, by supplying low and zero-carbon marine fuels including biofuels, methanol, ammonia, and potentially hydrogen, while enabling green technologies such as carbon capture, storage and utilisation."
4. **Singapore Registry of Ships:** "Recognise and incentivise owners to operate green ships, working towards 50% of the SRS fleet to be green ships by 2050."
5. **Efforts at IMO and other international platforms:** "Standard-setter and bridge builder, advocating for strong, credible, and inclusive climate action at the IMO and international fora."
6. **Research and development and talent:** "Global hub for maritime decarbonisation R&D, enabled by a vibrant ecosystem with talent and expertise to develop and deploy innovations."
7. **Carbon awareness, carbon accounting and green financing:** "Green maritime finance hub by promoting green financing landscape and strengthening carbon accounting and reporting."

*Source: COS 2022 Media Factsheet, MPA, 09 March 2022. https://www.mpa.gov.sg/media-centre/details/cos-2022---media-factsheet---maritime-singapore-decarbonisation-blueprint-working-towards-2050

### 8.2 Harbour Craft New-Build Mandate (2030)

The September 2025 MPA-SSA Briefing confirmed that "from 2030 onwards, all new harbour crafts must meet one of three criteria: they must be fully electric, compatible with B100 biofuel, or net-zero fuels (NZFs) ready." *Source: MPA-SSA Briefing, SSA, 11 September 2025. https://www.ssa.org.sg/mpa-ssa-briefing-unites-harbour-craft-stakeholders-to-meet-decarbonisation-goals/

*Operational implication:* For the workspace routing cargo through Singapore that depends on harbour craft services (e.g., bunkering support craft, vessel-to-vessel transfer, lighterage, port service launches), this mandate affects the operational cost base of harbour craft operators from 2030 onwards.

The briefing also identified key transition challenges: "high-cost concerns and uncertainty in technology availability," each propulsion pathway presenting "unique technical and financial hurdles" — electric craft require "substantial upfront investment and reliable charging infrastructure"; B100 biofuels "pose supply chain uncertainties and retrofitting complexities"; and net-zero fuels "raise concerns around safety standards and operational feasibility with existing infrastructure." *Source: MPA-SSA Briefing, SSA, 11 September 2025. https://www.ssa.org.sg/mpa-ssa-briefing-unites-harbour-craft-stakeholders-to-meet-decarbonisation-goals/

### 8.3 Financial Commitment

"MPA will commit additional funds of at least $300 million to support initiatives outlined in the Blueprint." *Source: COS 2022 Media Factsheet, MPA, 09 March 2022. https://www.mpa.gov.sg/media-centre/details/cos-2022---media-factsheet---maritime-singapore-decarbonisation-blueprint-working-towards-2050

In 2024, MPA committed "another S$50 million to support the implementation of the refreshed MSGI." *Source: Maritime Singapore Green Initiative, MPA. https://www.mpa.gov.sg/maritime-singapore/sustainability/maritime-singapore-green-initiative

### 8.4 Methanol Bunkering — Market Development

MPA received "a total of 50 submissions" to its methanol EOI, with "40% of them proposed end-to-end solutions covering all three areas." The "demand for methanol as a marine fuel in Singapore can potentially exceed one million tons per annum (MTPA) before 2030." Projects in the EOI response "have the potential to supply over one MTPA of low-carbon methanol by 2030, subject to commercial decisions and global developments." *Source: Singapore gears up to meet net-zero needs of shipping, MPA, 17 April 2024. https://www.mpa.gov.sg/media-centre/details/singapore-gears-up-to-meet-net-zero-needs-of-shipping/

*Operational implication:* Methanol bunkering at commercial scale in Singapore is progressing toward a 2030 target. Workspaces with ocean freight operations calling Singapore and using methanol-capable vessels (or carriers that have ordered such vessels) should track Singapore's methanol regulatory framework publication, as it will govern the safety and operational parameters for methanol bunkering and affect vessel availability and fuel pricing on Singapore-calling trade lanes.

---

## Section 10 — Registration and Reporting Obligations

### Green Ship Programme — Voluntary Registration

The GSP is a voluntary programme. SRS-registered ships and ocean-going vessels calling Singapore that wish to claim port dues concessions must meet qualifying criteria. The programme runs from 1 January 2025 to 31 December 2027. Operators are directed to "Shipping Circular No. 12 of 2024 and Port Marine Circular No. 13 of 2024 for more information on the GSP." *Source: Maritime Singapore Green Initiative, MPA. https://www.mpa.gov.sg/maritime-singapore/sustainability/maritime-singapore-green-initiative

The workspace, as freight forwarder, does not directly register under the GSP. *Analytical inference:* The workspace's commercial exposure is via nominated carriers' registration status and resulting port dues savings or costs.

### Green Craft Programme — Registration for Port Dues Concession

Harbour craft operators seeking port dues concession under the GCP must register newbuild craft registered from 1 January 2025 to 31 December 2027. Operators are directed to "Port Marine Circular No. 15 of 2024 for more information on the GCP." *Source: Maritime Singapore Green Initiative, MPA. https://www.mpa.gov.sg/maritime-singapore/sustainability/maritime-singapore-green-initiative

*Legal Confirmation Required:* Where the workspace has any operational interest in MPA-licensed harbour craft in Singapore, eligibility and registration requirements under the GCP require review of Port Marine Circular No. 15 of 2024.

### Carbon Accounting Reporting Obligations

The Blueprint's Focus Area 7 and the Green Awareness Programme (GAP) address "raising awareness on and developing industry capabilities in carbon accounting and management" under a "Carbon Management Memorandum of Understanding (MoU) signed between MPA, the United Nations Global Compact Network Singapore (GCNS), and various industry associations." *Source: Maritime Singapore Green Initiative, MPA. https://www.mpa.gov.sg/maritime-singapore/sustainability/maritime-singapore-green-initiative

*Specific mandatory reporting thresholds or filing requirements for freight forwarders or cargo principals under this MoU not available from primary sources as of June 2026.* The programme is described in the source as driven by the GAP, which focuses on raising awareness rather than imposing mandatory filing requirements.

---

## Section 11 — Operational System Requirements

### 11.1 For Workspaces with Singapore Harbour Craft Interests (2030 Mandate)

**Scope:** Any workspace holding or commissioning harbour craft services directly in Singapore.
**Deadline:** From 2030 onwards — new harbour craft new-build.
**Gap from current state:** *Analytical inference:* If the workspace's Singapore port operations rely on third-party harbour craft operators whose fleet consists entirely of conventional diesel craft, those operators face new-build requirements from 2030 and potentially higher operating costs on legacy fleet assets. No current monitoring mechanism for counterparty decarbonisation compliance status has been identified as standard practice in the source blocks.

**Action:** Build counterparty decarbonisation tracking for Singapore harbour craft service providers into commercial due diligence from 2026 onwards, ahead of the 2030 deadline.

### 11.2 Carrier Selection and Green Fuel Premium Tracking

**Scope:** Workspaces routing ocean freight through Singapore on any trade lane.
**Deadline:** Ongoing — GSP/GCP incentive window closes 31 December 2027; methanol bunkering framework publication pending.
**Gap from current state:** *Analytical inference:* Most freight forwarder procurement systems do not currently track carrier enrolment in MPA's MSGI programmes or carrier fleet Carbon Intensity Indicator (CII) ratings. As Singapore's incentive structure evolves, carriers with qualifying green status will have different port-dues cost profiles than non-enrolled carriers, which may affect freight rate competitiveness on Singapore-calling services.

**Action:** Add MPA MSGI enrolment status and CII rating to carrier qualification criteria for Singapore-routing RFQs from 2026 onwards.

### 11.3 Client-Facing Carbon Accounting Capability

**Scope:** Workspaces whose clients request Singapore port emissions data as part of Scope 3 reporting under external frameworks (e.g., CSRD, ISSB S2, or GHG Protocol).
**Deadline:** Driven by client contractual requirements, not Singapore regulation directly.
**Gap from current state:** *Analytical inference:* The Blueprint's Focus Area 7 (carbon accounting) and the MPA-GCNS MoU are positioning Singapore to become a hub for green maritime finance and carbon reporting. As this infrastructure matures, workspaces that already have Singapore-route emissions data capabilities will be better positioned to respond to client requests without delay.

**Action:** Review current Singapore port-call emissions data collection practices against IMO-standard emissions factors and MPA's developing carbon accounting guidance.

### 11.4 Workforce Training — Green Vessel Operations

The Workforce Singapore Career Conversion Programme (CCP) for Sea Transport "offers salary support for reskilling local talent in green vessel operations" with competencies including "IoT fleet management and predictive maintenance" and "carbon accounting and battery systems management." *Source: MPA-SSA Briefing, SSA, 11 September 2025. https://www.ssa.org.sg/mpa-ssa-briefing-unites-harbour-craft-stakeholders-to-meet-decarbonisation-goals/

*Operational implication:* Workspaces with Singapore-based maritime operations staff (operations, port coordination, vessel management) may find the CCP relevant for workforce development planning as green operations skills become increasingly required.

---

## Section 13 — Adjacent Industry Research and Alternatives

### IMO-MPA NextGEN Initiative

Singapore participates in "the joint IMO-MPA NextGEN initiative (where 'GEN' stands for 'Green and Efficient Navigation')" targeting least-developed countries and small island developing states to support their green maritime transition. *Source: Singapore gears up to meet net-zero needs of shipping, MPA, 17 April 2024. https://www.mpa.gov.sg/media-centre/details/singapore-gears-up-to-meet-net-zero-needs-of-shipping/

*Analytical inference:* For workspaces routing cargo through least-developed-country ports or small island state corridors, this initiative is building future-port green standards capacity that may affect port-call requirements on those routes over the medium term.

### MPA-IEA Memorandum of Understanding

"MPA and the International Energy Agency (IEA) signed an MoU on the Energy Transition of the Maritime and Port Industries." The MoU "will enable the sharing of best practices across maritime and energy industries, support the adoption and transition of zero and near-zero emission fuels by these industries through capacity building training programmes, and contribute to relevant fuel-related projects and initiatives." *Source: Singapore gears up to meet net-zero needs of shipping, MPA, 17 April 2024. https://www.mpa.gov.sg/media-centre/details/singapore-gears-up-to-meet-net-zero-needs-of-shipping/

*Analytical inference:* The MPA-IEA partnership strengthens Singapore's position as a maritime fuel transition intelligence hub. IEA analysis products on maritime energy transitions, informed by Singapore data, will be a credible source for forward bunker fuel cost and availability modelling on Singapore-calling trade lanes.

### Port Authorities Roundtable (PAR)

The Blueprint page references the "Port Authorities Roundtable (PAR)" and "PAR2025@Osaka" as an active international coordination forum. *Source: Maritime Singapore Decarbonisation Blueprint, MPA. https://www.mpa.gov.sg/maritime-singapore/sustainability/maritime-singapore-decarbonisation-blueprint

*Analytical inference:* PAR is a potential source of multilateral green port standards that could extend Singapore's decarbonisation requirements into a wider port network. Workspaces operating on Asia-Europe and transpacific corridors where PAR member ports cluster should monitor PAR outputs.

---

prior.full_brief:
# TxDOT Freight Planning: Statewide Framework and Resources

## Market Signal Brief

---

# Purpose and Context

This brief covers the Texas Department of Transportation (TxDOT) statewide freight planning framework, with particular focus on the 2027 Texas Freight Mobility Plan (TFMP) update currently in development, the Texas Connected Freight Corridors technology deployment program, and the Texas Emissions Reduction Plan (TERP) grant program. The framework operates at the intersection of federal compliance requirements, state infrastructure investment, and regional corridor-level implementation. For workspaces operating road-primary freight movements across Texas trade lanes — including cross-border Mexico-US corridors, Gulf Coast port access, and the Texas Triangle intermodal network — this framework determines which corridors receive investment priority, which technology deployments affect drayage and long-haul operations, and which grant mechanisms can offset fleet compliance costs.

Severity: MONITORING. No immediate compliance deadline is imposed on private freight operators by the TFMP framework itself; however, the 2027 plan update is in active development with stakeholder engagement windows open now through late 2027, and the Texas Connected Freight Corridors deployment is an active infrastructure program affecting operational conditions on the Texas Triangle.

Re-check window: Mid-2026 (stakeholder engagement phase opens; freight bottleneck and network assessments begin per published timeline).

---

# What's Moving and What Triggered It

**The 2027 Texas Freight Mobility Plan update is in active development.** FACT: "TxDOT is developing its 2027 TFMP. This will be the four-year update of its 2023 plan, in accordance with requirements in the Fixing America's Surface Transportation Act and 49 USC 70202." *Source: TxDOT – Texas Freight Mobility Plan (2027 Update Detail Page), TxDOT, 2025–2026. https://www.txdot.gov/projects/planning/freight-planning/texas-freight-mobility-plan.html

The trigger for the update is statutory: the FAST Act and 49 USC 70202 establish a four-year update cycle for state freight plans as a condition of federal compliance. FACT: The 2027 TFMP is described as a four-year update of the 2023 plan "in accordance with requirements in the Fixing America's Surface Transportation Act and 49 USC 70202." *Source: TxDOT – Texas Freight Mobility Plan (2027 Update Detail Page), TxDOT, 2025–2026. https://www.txdot.gov/projects/planning/freight-planning/texas-freight-mobility-plan.html

Federal recognition of Texas's state freight plan is established at the USDOT level. FACT: The USDOT page for the Texas Freight Plan hosts the document "texas-delivers-2050.pdf" and was last updated Wednesday, December 6, 2023. *Source: U.S. DOT – Texas Freight Plan (Federal Recognition Page), USDOT, December 6, 2023. https://www.transportation.gov/mission/office-secretary/office-policy/freight/freight-infrastructure-and-policy/texas-freight-plan

**The state's economic scale drives the planning urgency.** FACT: "If Texas were a nation, it would be the 8th largest economy in the world. It has been the #1 U.S. exporter for 23 years in a row, and it is the largest energy-producing state in the Nation." *Source: TxDOT – Texas Freight Mobility Plan (2027 Update Detail Page), TxDOT, 2025–2026. https://www.txdot.gov/projects/planning/freight-planning/texas-freight-mobility-plan.html

Analytical inference: For workspaces with road-primary operations on Texas corridors, the 2027 TFMP update is the primary vehicle through which state-level infrastructure investment priorities, bottleneck remediation, and technology deployment programs will be codified for the 2027–2031 planning cycle. Operators that engage the stakeholder process during 2026 are positioned to influence corridor prioritization and project sequencing.

---

# Who's Driving It and What They Want

**TxDOT is the lead agency**, operating through its Freight Planning division. Contact: TxDOT_Freight@txdot.gov, 737-219-9044, 125 East 11th Street, Austin, TX 78701. *Source: TxDOT – Texas Freight Mobility Plan (2027 Update Detail Page), TxDOT, 2025–2026. https://www.txdot.gov/projects/planning/freight-planning/texas-freight-mobility-plan.html

**The Texas Freight Advisory Committee (TFAC)** is a named stakeholder body in the engagement process. FACT: "The engagement plan includes interaction with, but not limited to, the Texas Freight Advisory Committee and Metropolitan Planning Organization freight advisory committees in the state, stakeholder workshops, interviews, project communications, surveys, and project webpage contents." *Source: TxDOT – Texas Freight Mobility Plan (2027 Update Detail Page), TxDOT, 2025–2026. https://www.txdot.gov/projects/planning/freight-planning/texas-freight-mobility-plan.html

**Metropolitan Planning Organizations (MPOs)** are active participants at the regional level. The El Paso MPO provides a documented example of regional implementation: FACT: "El Paso MPO staff is actively participating in the freight plans for New Mexico and Texas, Including identifying critical corridors at state and local level. As part of the 2022 Texas Freight Plan development, MPO staff has submitted to TxDOT six proposed Critical Urban Freight Corridors (CUFC)." *Source: El Paso MPO – Freight Planning, El Paso MPO, accessed 2026. https://www.elpasompo.org/Freight

**Private freight partners** are participants in the Texas Connected Freight Corridors program. FACT: "The Texas Connected Freight Corridors Project is a collaborative effort with public and private stakeholders to deploy connected vehicle technologies to more than 1,000 commercial vehicles to improve traveler information, asset condition management, and system performance." *Source: TxDOT – Texas Connected Freight Corridors, TxDOT, accessed 2026. https://www.txdot.gov/about/programs/innovative-transportation/texas-connected-freight-corridors.html

Analytical inference: The engagement architecture — TFAC, MPO freight advisory committees, stakeholder workshops, supply chain working groups — creates multiple documented entry points for freight operators to register operational challenges and corridor priorities. Workspaces moving freight on the Texas Triangle or across the Mexico-US border at El Paso have specific named channels (MPO CUFC submission process, TFAC participation) through which route-level concerns can be elevated into the statewide plan.

---

# Expected Trajectory and Conversion Triggers

**The 2027 TFMP has a published, phased development timeline.** FACT:

- "Late 2025 through Late 2027 – Public involvement"
- "Late 2025 – Project kickoff, data assessment, analysis of trends, disruptors, and opportunities."
- "Early 2026 – Multimodal data inventory, conduct a data needs assessment, supply chain review, critical freight facility assessment."
- "Mid 2026 – Begin stakeholder engagement, conduct a freight bottleneck and network assessments."
- "Late 2026 – Document economic importance of multimodal freight, finalize Texas freight mobility recommendations, finalize implementation plan."

*Source: TxDOT – Texas Freight Mobility Plan (2027 Update Detail Page), TxDOT, 2025–2026. https://www.txdot.gov/projects/planning/freight-planning/texas-freight-mobility-plan.html

**The plan's stated outcome categories** are: policies, programs, technology and operations investments, and capital projects. FACT: "The development of the 2027 Texas Freight Mobility Plan culminates in policy, program, technology and operations, and project recommendations." *Source: TxDOT – Texas Freight Mobility Plan (2027 Update Detail Page), TxDOT, 2025–2026. https://www.txdot.gov/projects/planning/freight-planning/texas-freight-mobility-plan.html

**The Texas Connected Freight Corridors program defines the technology conversion trigger**: connected vehicle deployment to over 1,000 commercial vehicles on the Texas Triangle. FACT: "The project covers the 865-mile Texas Triangle of I-35, including extension to Laredo, I-45, and I-10 linking Austin, Dallas-Fort Worth, Houston, and San Antonio." *Source: TxDOT – Texas Connected Freight Corridors, TxDOT, accessed 2026. https://www.txdot.gov/about/programs/innovative-transportation/texas-connected-freight-corridors.html

Analytical inference: The conversion from planning signal to infrastructure reality occurs in two waves: (1) Late 2026, when final TFMP recommendations are published and project investment decisions are codified; (2) ongoing, as connected vehicle technology deployment on the Texas Triangle progressively changes operational conditions for trucks on I-35 (Laredo corridor), I-45, and I-10. For workspaces with Laredo or El Paso border crossings, the I-35 extension to Laredo and I-10 are the corridors most directly affected.

**TERP grant cycles are the near-term financial conversion trigger.** FACT: "Since 2001, TERP has provided thousands of Texans with grant funding to purchase new vehicles and equipment." FACT: "To date, TERP has distributed over $1.4 billion in grants to reduce NOx emissions across Texas." *Source: Texas Commission on Environmental Quality – Texas Emissions Reduction Plan (TERP), TCEQ, last modified 2026-06-03. https://www.tceq.texas.gov/airquality/terp

Specific [open grant dates and current funding availability] not available from primary sources as of 2026-06-03 — the TCEQ TERP page states "There are no open grants at this time" and directs operators to join the TERP Hot Topics email list for updates.

---

# Operational and Cost Implications If It Materializes

**For road-primary operations on the Texas Triangle (I-35, I-45, I-10):**

FACT: The Texas Connected Freight Corridors program goals include to "Reduce truck-related crashes," "Reduce the time trucks spend in congestion," and achieve vehicle interaction with traffic signals to "reduce stops and truck idling time." *Source: TxDOT – Texas Connected Freight Corridors, TxDOT, accessed 2026. https://www.txdot.gov/about/programs/innovative-transportation/texas-connected-freight-corridors.html

Analytical inference: Reduced idling and congestion time on the Texas Triangle — specifically I-35 from Dallas-Fort Worth through San Antonio to Laredo — translates to measurable fuel savings and dwell-time reductions for road drayage and long-haul operations. Workspaces routing high-value or time-sensitive cargo through Laredo (the busiest US-Mexico land port by value) are the most operationally exposed to these infrastructure changes, in both directions: congestion relief is an upside; the technology transition period introduces uncertainty in transit time modeling.

**For fleet operators with older vehicles operating in Texas:**

FACT: TERP "offers grants for people and businesses in industries like trucking, farming, and construction" including the "DERI Rebate Grant Program" for on-road haul truck tractors and the "Texas Natural Gas Vehicle Grant Program" offering grants "to repower heavy-duty or medium-duty vehicles with natural gas engines or replace the vehicles with natural gas engines." *Source: Texas Commission on Environmental Quality – Texas Emissions Reduction Plan (TERP), TCEQ, last modified 2026-06-03. https://www.tceq.texas.gov/airquality/terp

Analytical inference: For workspaces that own or manage road fleet assets operating in Texas — including drayage from Gulf Coast ports or cross-border linehaul — the TERP grant programs represent a funded pathway to fleet modernization that offsets capital cost. The DERI program targets haul truck tractors directly. The fact that no grants are currently open means the action is to register for TERP Hot Topics to capture the next opening cycle rather than to apply immediately.

**For multimodal operations including air and ocean:**

The TFMP's scope is explicitly multimodal. FACT: "The TFMP will capture Texas freight transportation assets, conditions, current and future needs, and economic importance through the lens of critical supply chains as well as broader findings related to freight operations and international trade." *Source: TxDOT – Texas Freight Mobility Plan (2027 Update Detail Page), TxDOT, 2025–2026. https://www.txdot.gov/projects/planning/freight-planning/texas-freight-mobility-plan.html

Analytical inference: For air-primary workspaces moving cargo through DFW or Houston (both on the Texas Triangle), the TFMP's multimodal supply chain review (scheduled early 2026) is expected to address first- and last-mile connectivity from airports to highway freight corridors. The critical freight facility assessment is the mechanism through which airport cargo facility constraints may be elevated to the statewide plan.

**For cross-border Mexico-US operations:**

The El Paso MPO's CUFC designation process illustrates the mechanism by which border corridor roads are nominated for prioritized investment. FACT: The El Paso MPO submitted "six proposed Critical Urban Freight Corridors (CUFC)" to TxDOT "taking into consideration" four categories including corridors that "Connect an intermodal facility to the Primary Highway Freight System (PHFS)" and corridors "important to the movement of freight within the region." *Source: El Paso MPO – Freight Planning, El Paso MPO, accessed 2026. https://www.elpasompo.org/Freight

Analytical inference: Workspaces routing cross-border cargo through El Paso have a documented regional planning participant (the El Paso MPO) actively advocating for CUFC designations. The 2027 TFMP update's stakeholder engagement phase (mid-2026 onwards) is the next formal window to reinforce those corridor priorities.

---

# Competitive Implications

Analytical inference: The competitive dynamic in Texas-routed freight is shaped by which operators have visibility into the TFMP stakeholder process and which do not. Operators that engage TFAC, MPO freight advisory committees, or TxDOT directly during the mid-2026 stakeholder engagement phase can register operational bottlenecks — corridor-specific dwell times, border wait times, last-mile constraints from ports — that influence the plan's final project and program recommendations. Operators without that engagement are passive recipients of whoever else's priorities shaped the plan.

Analytical inference: The Texas Connected Freight Corridors program, deploying connected vehicle technology to over 1,000 commercial vehicles on the Texas Triangle, is a freight-partner-participation program. Workspaces whose road fleet partners are already enrolled in the program receive earlier access to real-time traffic and hazard information on I-35, I-45, and I-10. Workspaces whose carriers are not enrolled operate with a less complete operational picture during the deployment window.

Analytical inference: TERP grant access is first-come, first-served for some programs (the Texas Natural Gas Vehicle Grant Program explicitly operates on this basis per the TCEQ page). Workspaces that monitor TERP grant openings and apply early in a cycle gain fleet modernization funding that competitors who miss the cycle do not. The $1.4 billion distributed to date signals a sustained, multi-cycle program — but individual grant windows are time-limited.

No competitor-specific named freight operator positioning is available from the source pool. Named competitor analysis is a research gap requiring industry trade reporting.

---

# Client Conversation Talking Points

**What the workspace can credibly say:**

- Texas is the US's largest exporter by value, operating as effectively the world's eighth-largest economy; supply chain reliability in Texas corridors is not a marginal concern but a structural one for any client moving goods to, from, or within the state.
- TxDOT is in an active four-year planning cycle (2025–2027) that will set infrastructure investment priorities through 2031 and beyond; the stakeholder engagement process is open and accepting input now.
- The Texas Triangle (I-35/I-45/I-10) is the subject of an active connected vehicle deployment program targeting over 1,000 commercial vehicles; truck operators on these corridors can expect progressive improvements in real-time hazard notification and signal optimization at distribution centers.
- Texas operates one of the most substantial state-level fleet grant programs in the US (TERP, $1.4 billion distributed since 2001), directly relevant to clients managing their own Texas-based fleet or evaluating carrier sustainability credentials.

**What to avoid saying:**

- Do not claim specific TERP grant amounts, timelines, or application windows without checking the current TCEQ TERP page at https://www.tceq.texas.gov/airquality/terp — grant availability changes on a cycle-by-cycle basis.
- Do not characterize the 2027 TFMP recommendations as settled — the plan is in development; recommendations are not finalized until late 2026.
- Do not assert that connected vehicle technology deployment on the Texas Triangle is complete — the program page describes it as in active development, not fully deployed.
- Do not cite the USDOT Notable Practices in State Carbon Reduction Strategies PDF — the source block confirmed access denied to that document; no claims from it can be grounded.

**Questions to pose to clients:**

- Which Texas corridors carry the client's highest-volume or most time-sensitive freight, and have those corridors been nominated as CUFCs through the relevant MPO?
- Does the client's road fleet (or their carriers' fleets) include vehicles eligible for TERP grant programs? The DERI Rebate Program targets haul truck tractors directly.
- Is the client currently engaged with TFAC or their regional MPO freight advisory committee? If not, the mid-2026 stakeholder engagement phase is the next entry point.

---

# What the Workspace Should Do Now

**Action 1 — Register for TERP grant notifications (road fleet operations).** MONITORING → COST ALERT on next opening. Subscribe to the TERP Hot Topics email list at https://www.tceq.texas.gov/airquality/terp. The program targets trucking, construction, and agricultural fleet operators. No grants are currently open; the subscription provides advance notice of next-cycle openings. Relevant for workspaces managing or advising on road fleet assets in Texas, particularly haul truck tractors and heavy-duty vehicles on the Texas Triangle or border corridors.

**Action 2 — Engage the 2027 TFMP stakeholder process at mid-2026 opening.** The bottleneck assessment and stakeholder engagement phase is scheduled to begin mid-2026 per TxDOT's published timeline. Contact TxDOT_Freight@txdot.gov to register interest in stakeholder workshops or supply chain working groups. This is the formal channel through which corridor-specific operational bottlenecks (dwell times, last-mile gaps, port connector deficiencies) can be elevated to the statewide plan.

**Action 3 — Map road carrier participation in Texas Connected Freight Corridors.** Determine whether primary road carriers operating on I-35 (including the Laredo extension), I-45, or I-10 are enrolled as freight partners in the Texas Connected Freight Corridors Project. The program is deploying to over 1,000 commercial vehicles; carriers already enrolled receive earlier access to real-time corridor intelligence. Contact information for the program is available at https://www.txdot.gov/about/programs/innovative-transportation/texas-connected-freight-corridors.html.

**Action 4 — Track El Paso MPO CUFC designations for border corridor planning.** For workspaces with cross-border Mexico-US operations routed through El Paso, the El Paso MPO is the active regional planning participant. Monitor the MPO's freight planning page at https://www.elpasompo.org/Freight for updates to CUFC designations and TxDOT coordination activity in the 2027 TFMP cycle. The MPO also maintains binational cooperation and ports-of-entry data relevant to cross-border freight.

---

prior.full_brief:
# Sustainable Air Freight Alliance (SAFA) — Market Signal Brief

## Purpose and Scope of This Document

This brief covers the Sustainable Air Freight Alliance (SAFA), a BSR-facilitated multi-stakeholder initiative to track and reduce carbon dioxide emissions from air freight. The brief is anchored to workspaces operating in air-primary freight forwarding across the Americas, Europe, and Asia, with cargo verticals including live events, fine art, luxury goods, film and TV, high-value automotive, and humanitarian. It synthesises the BSR launch source, the Delta Cargo membership announcement, IATA RP1678 measurement infrastructure, and the 2024 IATA–Smart Freight Centre partnership. The Air Cargo News source was inaccessible (Cloudflare security block); claims that would have drawn from it are omitted. The IATA RP1678 programme page was also inaccessible (Cloudflare security block); supporting context on that standard is drawn from the IATA CO2 Connect page and the IATA–SFC press release instead.

---

# 1. What's Moving and What Triggered It

MONITORING

**What SAFA is and what triggered its formation**

FACT: BSR launched the Sustainable Air Freight Alliance (SAFA), described as "a forum for buyers and suppliers alike to collaborate on air freight emissions reduction," following an incubation period with active contribution from over 15 companies. *Source: 'Improve Sustainability with New Air Freight Alliance,' BSR, February 5, 2019. https://www.bsr.org/en/blog/improve-sustainability-with-new-air-freight-alliance

FACT: The trigger BSR identified was structural — "there remains no consistent forum for direct business-to-business exchange between the various stakeholders, which is key to driving collaborative solutions. There is also a general lack of transparency around the application and use of the environmental performance information requested from airlines by shippers for their reporting and decision-making needs." *Source: BSR, February 5, 2019. https://www.bsr.org/en/blog/improve-sustainability-with-new-air-freight-alliance

FACT: The sectoral backdrop BSR named at launch: "air transport represents around two percent of global carbon dioxide emissions. However, the air freight sector is projected to grow at five percent per year until 2050—faster than any other mode—and with potential to increase emissions significantly." *Source: BSR, February 5, 2019. https://www.bsr.org/en/blog/improve-sustainability-with-new-air-freight-alliance

FACT: SAFA is facilitated by BSR (Business for Social Responsibility), described as "a global nonprofit business network and consultancy dedicated to sustainability." *Source: 'Delta Cargo Joins the Sustainable Air Freight Alliance,' Supply & Demand Chain Executive, July 23, 2020. https://www.sdcexec.com/sustainability/press-release/21139757/delta-group-delta-cargo-joins-the-sustainable-air-freight-alliance

FACT: "SAFA is one of over 20 collaborative initiatives facilitated and managed by BSR, involving over 400 companies in total." *Source: Supply & Demand Chain Executive, July 23, 2020. https://www.sdcexec.com/sustainability/press-release/21139757/delta-group-delta-cargo-joins-the-sustainable-air-freight-alliance

**Measurement standard context**

FACT: IATA and the Smart Freight Centre (SFC) announced, on March 13, 2024, "a partnership to provide consistent and transparent CO2 emissions calculations for air cargo shipments," focused on developing the cargo component of IATA's CO2 Connect offering. *Source: 'IATA and Smart Freight Centre Join Forces on CO2 Emission Calculations,' IATA Press Release, March 13, 2024. https://www.iata.org/en/pressroom/2024-releases/2024-03-13-02/

FACT: The IATA CO2 Connect for Cargo calculator is "based on industry-approved Recommended Practice 1678" and "aligns with the Global Logistics Emissions Council (GLEC) in their framework for logistics emissions calculations methodologies, ISO 14083, as well as EcoTransIT & German DIN Standards." *Source: IATA CO2 Connect for Cargo page, IATA. https://www.iata.org/en/services/data/environment-sustainability/co2-connect/for-cargo/

FACT: RP1678 "is the global standard to measure air cargo carbon footprint at the shipment level; it is also recognized as the reference methodology for air cargo by the Global Logistics Emissions Council (GLEC) in their framework for logistics emissions calculation methodologies." The methodology "was developed by the IATA Air Cargo Carbon Footprint (ACCF) working group and endorsed by ICAO's Committee on Aviation Environmental Protection (CAEP) in 2016." *Source: IATA–SFC Press Release, March 13, 2024. https://www.iata.org/en/pressroom/2024-releases/2024-03-13-02/

*Analytical inference:* The convergence of RP1678 with ISO 14083 and the GLEC Framework is directly relevant to workspaces operating in air-primary freight forwarding, because shipper clients reporting under ISO 14083 or CSRD-adjacent Scope 3 frameworks will increasingly expect forwarder-supplied emissions data calculated to the same standard — creating an implicit data-quality pressure on the workspace even without formal SAFA membership.

---

# 2. Who's Driving It and What They Want

**Founding and early members (sourced)**

FACT: "Participants include DB Schenker, DHL, Finnair, GEODIS, H&M, Maersk, Nike, and United Airlines," named at the time SAFA opened for membership. *Source: BSR, February 5, 2019. https://www.bsr.org/en/blog/improve-sustainability-with-new-air-freight-alliance

FACT: Delta Cargo joined SAFA, with the announcement dated July 23, 2020, described as aligning "with Delta's pledge earlier this year to become the world's first carbon neutral airline," and a commitment of "$1 billion over the next 10 years to mitigate all emissions from its global business." *Source: Supply & Demand Chain Executive, July 23, 2020. https://www.sdcexec.com/sustainability/press-release/21139757/delta-group-delta-cargo-joins-the-sustainable-air-freight-alliance

FACT: Delta Cargo stated it was "working with its counterparts through SAFA to share best practices and create common goals and metrics." *Source: Supply & Demand Chain Executive, July 23, 2020. https://www.sdcexec.com/sustainability/press-release/21139757/delta-group-delta-cargo-joins-the-sustainable-air-freight-alliance

FACT: On the supply-chain measurement infrastructure side, Kuehne+Nagel is identified as the pilot customer for IATA CO2 Connect for Cargo, working with IATA and SFC "to ensure that the tool meets the needs and requirements of freight forwarders and their customers." *Source: IATA–SFC Press Release, March 13, 2024. https://www.iata.org/en/pressroom/2024-releases/2024-03-13-02/

FACT: The SFC Clean Air Transport program, which partners with IATA on measurement, "comprises over 50 members, including airlines, freight forwarders, shippers and fuel providers." *Source: IATA–SFC Press Release, March 13, 2024. https://www.iata.org/en/pressroom/2024-releases/2024-03-13-02/

**Stated interests and strategy**

FACT: BSR's stated rationale for SAFA is to enable companies to: "Build dialogue with business partners to understand needs and strengthen long-term business relationships; Demonstrate company and industry leadership on sustainability; Reduce exposure to risks (such as regulations) to the air freight industry by leveraging collective knowledge and action; Credibly measure and report their emissions performance; Help shape international standards, in line with ICAO, IATA's work, and sectorial initiatives; Access a collaborative forum for sharing sustainability information, best practices for reducing GHG emissions, and innovation opportunities." *Source: BSR, February 5, 2019. https://www.bsr.org/en/blog/improve-sustainability-with-new-air-freight-alliance

*Analytical inference:* The membership set — major forwarders (DB Schenker, GEODIS), large integrators (DHL), major shippers (H&M, Nike, Maersk), and network carriers (Finnair, United, Delta) — reflects a supply-chain-wide coalition structure rather than a carrier-only or shipper-only lobbying group. The participation of Maersk (a multimodal operator with significant air cargo ambitions) alongside freight forwarders signals that multimodal operators view air freight emissions measurement as a near-term commercial differentiator.

*Analytical inference:* For workspaces acting as freight forwarder across the live events, fine art, luxury goods, and film and TV verticals, the significance is that their shipper clients — particularly large consumer brands and event producers — may already be SAFA members or SAFA-adjacent (through SFC membership or ISO 14083 reporting obligations), and will request per-shipment CO2 data at the standard the SAFA ecosystem defines.

---

# 3. Expected Trajectory and Conversion Triggers

MONITORING — re-check window: Q4 2025 (IATA CO2 Connect for Cargo SAF accounting integration); ongoing for SAFA membership growth and any formal reporting mandates referencing RP1678.

**Measurement infrastructure: near-term timeline (sourced)**

FACT: The IATA CO2 Connect for Cargo API solution was described as available "early 2025," with "early pilot testing available from October 2024 for a limited group." *Source: IATA CO2 Connect for Cargo page, IATA. https://www.iata.org/en/services/data/environment-sustainability/co2-connect/for-cargo/

FACT: IATA CO2 Connect for Cargo was described in the March 2024 press release as available "as of Q4 2024." *Source: IATA–SFC Press Release, March 13, 2024. https://www.iata.org/en/pressroom/2024-releases/2024-03-13-02/

FACT: "In 2025, IATA CO2 Connect will account for SAF reductions on a network-wide basis and later look into implementing SAF reductions on an origin-destination basis." *Source: IATA CO2 Connect for Cargo page, IATA. https://www.iata.org/en/services/data/environment-sustainability/co2-connect/for-cargo/

*Analytical inference:* The SAF accounting integration scheduled for 2025 is a conversion trigger for workspaces: once per-shipment CO2 data carries SAF reduction credits that are auditable at the origin-destination level, shipper clients will be able to see — and request — SAF-attributed emissions reductions on specific lanes. For air-primary workspaces carrying high-value time-sensitive cargo (live events, fine art, film and TV equipment), this creates a foreseeable lane-level sustainability differentiation moment.

**What would convert SAFA from signal to active commercial pressure**

*Analytical inference:* Three triggers, in increasing certainty order:

1. **Regulatory mandate referencing RP1678 or ISO 14083 for air cargo:** EU CSRD Scope 3 reporting requirements, which are in force for large EU companies and approaching for mid-caps, reference logistics emissions. *Legal Confirmation Required:* whether CSRD reporting for large EU-headquartered clients of the workspace formally requires RP1678-aligned data from forwarders is a question for legal and compliance counsel, but the directional pressure is clear.
2. **SAFA member shipper contract clauses:** As SAFA member shippers (Nike, H&M, and others) embed sustainability data requirements into freight procurement, non-SAFA forwarders who cannot supply RP1678-aligned per-shipment CO2 data face a tender disadvantage. *Specific threshold date not publicly available from primary sources as of 2026-06-09.*
3. **IATA CO2 Connect for Cargo reaching broad commercial availability:** With the tool described as available Q4 2024 and the API "early 2025," the infrastructure for standardised data supply is now operational. The conversion from voluntary to expected is driven by buyer-side uptake, which the sourced evidence does not quantify.

---

# 4. Operational and Cost Implications If It Materializes

**For workspaces operating as air-primary freight forwarder**

*Operational implication:* The workspace, in its role as freight forwarder, sits between airlines (data source) and shipper clients (data consumers). SAFA's design places the forwarder as a critical intermediary for per-shipment CO2 data. If the workspace does not have access to RP1678-aligned data per shipment, per airline, per aircraft type, it cannot fulfil data requests from shipper clients who are SAFA members or who report under ISO 14083 or CSRD Scope 3.

*Operational implication:* For cargo verticals where shipments are ad hoc and high-value (live events, fine art, film and TV, luxury goods, humanitarian), per-shipment CO2 calculation is particularly complex because load factors vary significantly by flight and aircraft type. IATA CO2 Connect for Cargo's approach — using "actual airline fuel consumption data, airline-specific cargo load factors, and airline-specific passenger numbers" — addresses this, but the workspace needs either direct access to the tool or data feeds from its airline partners who contribute data to it.

FACT: The IATA CO2 Connect for Cargo calculator "uses primary data from airlines" and "was developed in consultation with Smart Freight Centre, airlines, freight forwarders, shippers, and technology companies." *Source: IATA CO2 Connect for Cargo page, IATA. https://www.iata.org/en/services/data/environment-sustainability/co2-connect/for-cargo/

*Operational implication:* For the humanitarian vertical in particular, where cost scrutiny is high and donor reporting on supply chain emissions is increasingly required by institutional donors (ECHO, USAID, UN agencies), the ability to produce RP1678-aligned emissions data per shipment is becoming a procurement differentiator. *Specific cost of non-compliance or tender disadvantage is not publicly available from primary sources as of 2026-06-09.*

**Cost implications**

*Specific cost figures for SAFA membership fees, IATA CO2 Connect for Cargo licensing, or data integration costs are not publicly available from primary sources as of 2026-06-09.* These are classified as a research gap requiring direct inquiry with BSR and IATA.

---

# 5. Competitive Implications

**Named participants and their positioning**

FACT: DB Schenker and GEODIS are named founding SAFA participants, alongside DHL. *Source: BSR, February 5, 2019. https://www.bsr.org/en/blog/improve-sustainability-with-new-air-freight-alliance

FACT: Kuehne+Nagel is identified as the pilot customer for IATA CO2 Connect for Cargo. *Source: IATA–SFC Press Release, March 13, 2024. https://www.iata.org/en/pressroom/2024-releases/2024-03-13-02/

*Analytical inference:* DB Schenker, GEODIS, DHL, and Kuehne+Nagel — all direct competitors in air-primary freight forwarding — are either SAFA founding members or first-mover participants in the IATA CO2 Connect for Cargo pilot. This means these competitors have had multi-year lead time on: (a) contributing to standard-setting within SAFA, (b) developing internal RP1678-aligned emissions reporting workflows, and (c) positioning themselves in tender responses to SAFA member shippers as preferred sustainable carriers.

*Analytical inference:* For workspaces specialising in time-critical, high-value cargo verticals (live events, fine art, film and TV, high-value automotive), the competitive risk is not immediate loss of volume to SAFA members — it is a slow erosion of preference with brand-sensitive shipper clients who will increasingly weight sustainability credentials in freight procurement scoring. The conversion window is not yet closed, but the disadvantage compounds with time.

*Analytical inference:* COMPETITIVE EDGE opportunity: workspaces that join SAFA, or that independently deploy RP1678-aligned per-shipment CO2 reporting via IATA CO2 Connect for Cargo, can credibly position against the tier-one forwarders at the mid-market shipper segment — particularly fashion, consumer goods, and luxury brands where sustainability reporting is a board-level requirement but where the large forwarders may not offer tailored client-service reporting at the shipment level.

---

# 6. Client Conversation Talking Points

**What the workspace can credibly say**

- SAFA is a BSR-facilitated alliance of airlines, shippers, and freight forwarders designed to standardise and improve the transparency of air cargo emissions measurement. Named founding members include DB Schenker, DHL, GEODIS, Finnair, United, H&M, Nike, and Maersk.
- The measurement standard underpinning SAFA is IATA Recommended Practice 1678, which is also endorsed by ICAO CAEP and recognised by the GLEC Framework as the reference methodology for air cargo emissions — the same framework used in ISO 14083 logistics reporting. *Source: IATA–SFC Press Release, March 13, 2024. https://www.iata.org/en/pressroom/2024-releases/2024-03-13-02/
- IATA and the Smart Freight Centre launched a formal partnership in March 2024 to standardise air cargo CO2 calculations, with Kuehne+Nagel as the pilot freight forwarder. The IATA CO2 Connect for Cargo tool was described as available from Q4 2024, with an API from early 2025.
- For clients reporting Scope 3 emissions under CSRD or similar frameworks, per-shipment CO2 data aligned to RP1678 is the current industry standard for air freight. The workspace is actively assessing its data supply chain to ensure it can provide this.

**What the workspace should avoid saying**

- Do not claim SAFA membership or RP1678 compliance without verification that the workspace has formally joined or implemented the methodology. Overclaiming in a tender response to a SAFA member shipper carries reputational and contractual risk.
- Do not characterise SAFA as a regulatory requirement. *Legal Confirmation Required:* whether any regulatory framework formally mandates SAFA membership or RP1678 specifically (as opposed to ISO 14083 compliance, which aligns with it) requires counsel review.
- Do not cite SAF reduction credits in per-shipment emissions data until the workspace has confirmed which airlines it uses are contributing SAF accounting data to IATA CO2 Connect. The SAF network-wide accounting integration was described as a 2025 roadmap item, not confirmed as fully deployed.

**Questions to pose to airline partners**

- Are you contributing actual fuel consumption data to IATA CO2 Connect for Cargo?
- Can you provide RP1678-aligned per-shipment CO2 data via API to the workspace's reporting systems?
- What is your roadmap for SAF accounting at the origin-destination level, and when will that appear in CO2 Connect outputs?

---

# 7. What the Workspace Should Do Now

MONITORING → COMPETITIVE EDGE (conditional on actions below)

**Action 1: Assess SAFA membership**
Contact BSR directly to obtain current membership terms, fee structure, and active working-group calendar. SAFA is open for membership (stated at launch, no sourced evidence it is closed). *Specific cost: not publicly available from primary sources as of 2026-06-09.* Priority: air-primary operations team and commercial leadership jointly. Timing: within 30 days, before next freight tender cycle.

**Action 2: Audit per-shipment CO2 data capability by airline partner**
For each airline the workspace regularly uses on its primary lanes (Americas, Europe, Asia), confirm whether that airline contributes data to IATA CO2 Connect for Cargo and can supply RP1678-aligned per-shipment emissions data. This audit directly determines whether the workspace can fulfil data requests from SAFA-member shippers without a systems overhaul. Timing: 60 days.

**Action 3: Evaluate IATA CO2 Connect for Cargo access**
Request access information from IATA for the CO2 Connect for Cargo API (described as available from early 2025). Determine integration requirements with existing shipment management systems. For the high-value, time-sensitive verticals (live events, fine art, film and TV, luxury goods), shipment-level emissions data is a near-term client-service expectation, not a long-term aspiration. Timing: 60 days.

**Action 4: Add emissions data clause to airline service contracts**
*Legal Confirmation Required:* exact contract language for data provision obligations. Operationally, the workspace should instruct its operations and legal teams to add a clause requiring airline partners to provide RP1678-aligned per-shipment CO2 data as a standard service deliverable on air waybills, effective at next contract renewal. Without this clause, the workspace depends on airline goodwill rather than contractual entitlement to the data.

**Action 5: Position in the next relevant tender**
For any freight tender involving SAFA member shippers (consumer goods, fashion, luxury, large logistics buyers), the workspace should document its RP1678 alignment status, IATA CO2 Connect access, and SAFA engagement. Even without full membership, demonstrating awareness of and engagement with the standard is a differentiator at the mid-market segment not yet served by the large forwarder SAFA founding members.

**Re-check trigger for this brief:** Q4 2026 — reassess SAFA membership growth, any EU or national regulatory reference to RP1678 or SAFA, and IATA CO2 Connect SAF origin-destination accounting deployment status.

---
